summary:
Tepe Sarab (also written Teppe Sarab) is a small Neolithic site in the Kermanshah region of western Iran, situated in a highland valley of the Zagros Mountains at an elevation of approximately 1,500 meters. Excavated by Robert Braidwood's Oriental Institute team in the 1960s, the site dates to roughly 7000 to 6000 BCE and provides important evidence for early farming communities in the highland Zagros zone east of the main Mesopotamian farming heartland.

The occupants of Tepe Sarab herded sheep and goats — the primary domesticated animals at the site — alongside some cattle, and cultivated emmer wheat and other cereals. The faunal assemblage reflects the classic early Neolithic herding economy of the Zagros piedmont zone. The site produced notable clay figurines of animals and humans, adding to the wider pattern of figurine production in Neolithic Near Eastern communities. Though small in area and modest in architectural remains, Tepe Sarab contributes to understanding the geographic extent of the early agricultural transition across the highland zones flanking the Mesopotamian lowlands.

Tepe Sarab is often considered alongside other Zagros Neolithic sites such as [[event:1406|Jarmo]] and [[event:1476|Tepe Guran]] as part of a broader highland farming complex that developed somewhat independently from the lowland Hassuna-Samarra traditions of the Mesopotamian plain, reflecting the ecological and cultural diversity of early Neolithic communities in the greater Near East.
